event: acute-onset excruciating bilateral lower extremity pain
unprotected sexual intercourse two weeks prior to presentation
no history of tobacco, alcohol, or substance use
medication history included acetaminophen and ibuprofen as needed
previously prescribed azathioprine and prednisone for the autoimmune hepatitis
it was unclear when she last took immunosuppressive medication
had not followed up with gastroenterology for over eight months
received vaccination for influenza and COVID-19 four months prior
anasarca including the labia majora, labia minora, and bilateral lower extremities
pelvic exam demonstrated an erythematous vaginal vault with minimal discharge and no vesicles, lesions, or retained foreign objects
fine reticular violaceous patches developed on the right proximal thigh within several hours of presentation
total bilirubin 3.7 mg/dL with direct bilirubin 3.19 mg/dL
beta-human chorionic gonadotropin test was negative
paracentesis was done with peritoneal fluid analysis notable for leukocyte count 9821 with neutrophilic predominance (82 %)

CT angiography of the chest, abdomen, pelvis, and bilateral lower extremities revealed cirrhosis with portal hypertension, large volume abdominal ascites, splenomegaly, and generalized edematous wall thickening of the colon and rectum
required intubation due to labored breathing and tachypnea
early resuscitation was provided with intravenous fluids, blood products, and vasopressors
broad-spectrum antibiotic therapy was initiated including vancomycin, piperacillin-tazobactam, doxycycline, and clindamycin due to concern for possible toxic shock syndrome
received one dose of intravenous immunoglobulin
large violaceous non-blanching ecchymoses were present extending from the bilateral proximal thighs to the calves, and several flaccid bullae developed
the skin over the entire right leg, as well as the left proximal thigh to mid-shin, was dusky and violaceous with sharply demarcated borders adjacent to islands of unaffected skin
various-sized bullae up to greater than 10 cm filled with red to black-colored fluid were present
further laboratory tests included lactate dehydrogenase 298 units/L, fibrinogen 187 mg/dL, D-dimer > 20 mcg/mL, and urinalysis without signs of infection
salicylate level, acetaminophen level, Chlamydia trachomatis and Neisseria gonorrhea nucleic acid amplification tests, and urine toxicology screen were negative
blood culture from admission grew pan-susceptible Streptococcus pneumoniae
had progressive hypoxia and shock despite maximal ventilator and vasopressor support
passed away within 48 hours of admission
autopsy showed a cirrhotic liver with an entirely nodular surface, diffuse alveolar damage, and over five liters of serous fluid in the abdominal compartment
